core_takeaway: Agent_1 kept the first-turn Quill anchor, but Agent_5 converted a late Pencil claim into the unanimous baseline and had the larger top-item payoff.
why_or_counterexample: First speaking secured Agent_1's top item, but the final deal also gave Agent_5 its top item, worth 53 versus Agent_1's 39, so Agent_1 lost by 14.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by claiming Quill and asking others to allocate leftovers. Agent_5 later reframed the deal around Pencil-to-5 while preserving others' top claims, and Agents 1/3/4/5 converged on Baseline A despite Agent_2's Pencil concern.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Available for all five agents. The notes show Baseline A as the common fast path, with a Round-2 renegotiation idea mainly used to defuse Agent_2's objection over Pencil.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All five formal proposals submitted the same allocation: Agent_1 gets Quill and Agent_5 gets Pencil, with Agent_2 taking Apple.
phase_notes.private_voting: Available for 25 proposal-by-voter votes. Every vote accepted; Agent_1 accepted because Quill was secured, while Agent_5 accepted because Pencil gave its highest Round-1 utility.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection section appears in the transcript.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first public turn to make Quill the anchor and later treated any acceptable deal as one that left Quill with Agent_1. This gave Agent_1 its top item, but it only protected Agent_1's own 39-point claim.

evidence.quote: I’m aiming to secure Quill right away.
Quill stays with Agent_1, which is my priority.

mechanisms.theme: Late Pencil claim became the winning baseline
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 entered last in the public discussion but made a clear top-item demand for Pencil and proposed a complete allocation that preserved other agents' top claims. That demand became Baseline A and survived through proposal and voting.

evidence.quote: Baseline A puts Pencil with me

mechanisms.explanation: Other agents supported the allocation because it matched salient top items across the group, not because Agent_1 spoke first. This coalition logic made Agent_5's Pencil claim compatible with Agent_1's Quill anchor.

evidence.quote: It gives each agent their top item except for Apple
resolves the major priorities for everyone

mechanisms.theme: Formal proposal order did not differentiate outcomes
mechanisms.explanation: Every agent formally submitted the same allocation, and every proposal received unanimous acceptance. The last formal proposal was accepted by the system, but it was substantively identical to Agent_1's proposal, so formal ordering did not give Agent_1 an extra edge.

Both Agent_1 and Agent_5 received their top items, but Agent_5's top item was worth 53 while Agent_1's was worth 39. The counterexample is therefore not that Agent_1 failed to secure its claim, but that the last-position claim had higher utility.
